entries.intro: Prices have gone up again. I paid twice what I used to for the same sack of flour.
details: The merchants say it's the roads. Harder to move goods, so everything costs more.
I've started buying less and stretching what I have. Not the worst thing. I was probably wasteful before.
reactions: You tighten your belt and you carry on. What else can you do?

statCheck.pass: It's a supply problem, not greed. Fewer caravans coming through means less competition, and less competition means higher prices.
statCheck.fail: Couldn't tell you why things cost more. They just do.

entries.intro: A new merchant set up near the square selling dried herbs and spices. Hadn't seen half of them before.
details: I bought some kind of pepper that nearly took my head off. Good stuff though. A pinch goes a long way.
The merchant's friendly enough. Knows the name and use for everything on the table without looking.
reactions: Nice to have something new around here for a change.

entries.intro: I sold my extra wool at the market this morning. Got a fair price for once.
details: The buyer said the weavers have more orders than they can fill right now. Good timing on my part.
I put the coin toward a new pair of shears. The old ones have been dull since last season.
reactions: Not a bad day when you leave with more than you came with.

statCheck.pass: Wool prices always climb when the weavers are backed up. Sell now before they catch up on orders and the price drops.
statCheck.fail: I don't pretend to understand why prices go up and down. I just sell when it feels right.

entries.intro: Someone at the market tried to sell me a tool that was cracked right down the handle. Acted like I wouldn't notice.
details: I pointed it out and he shrugged, said it was cosmetic. A crack in a hatchet handle is not cosmetic.
I've seen him pull the same trick on other folks. The ones who don't look too closely get burned.
reactions: There's always one. You just have to check your goods before you hand over coin.

entries.intro: Trade around here has been good lately. Lots of people coming through, lots of goods moving.
details: I saw leather, iron stock, bolts of cloth. More variety than we usually get.
The baker's been selling twice as many loaves. Travelers need to eat, and her bread is the best around.
reactions: A busy market means a healthy settlement. I like hearing the noise.

statCheck.pass: The trade road got cleared up recently. That's what brought the traffic back. Good road means good business.
statCheck.fail: No idea why it picked up. I'm just glad it did.

entries.intro: Couldn't find salt anywhere today. Nobody had any.
details: The last trader who carried it said his supplier stopped delivering. Didn't say why.
I've still got a bit at home, but it won't last the month if I'm curing meat.
reactions: It's one of those things you don't think about until you can't get it.

statCheck.pass: Salt comes from the coast, and coast shipments are seasonal. Should see more once the dry weather settles the roads.
statCheck.fail: Beats me where the salt went. Somebody probably bought it all.

entries.intro: I traded some vegetables for a nice clay pot this morning. The potter seemed happy enough with the deal.
details: My old pot had a crack that leaked whenever I boiled anything. I'd been putting up with it for months.
The potter said she's got more than she can sell right now. Too many pots, not enough buyers.
reactions: Sometimes a fair swap is better than coin. Both sides walk away with something they needed.

entries.intro: The fruit at the market looked sorry this week. Bruised, small, not worth what they're asking.
details: I picked one up and it was soft enough to squeeze juice out of right there. Not in a good way.
The seller blamed the weather. Too much rain, too little sun. Could be true, I suppose.
reactions: I'll wait a week and check again. No point paying good coin for bad fruit.
